name: Sacred Geography
shortDescription: Sacred geography in Japanese mythology refers to specific locations believed to possess spiritual power or significance, often linked to kami and creation myths.
description: Sacred geography in Japanese mythology encompasses specific locations considered to be imbued with spiritual power or associated with significant mythological events, particularly the creation of Japan and the dwelling places of the kami (spirits or deities). These sites are not merely geographical landmarks but are active participants in the ongoing cosmological narrative. They serve as conduits for communication between the human and divine realms, and are often the focus of rituals, pilgrimages, and veneration. Mountains, waterfalls, rocks, trees, and islands can all be considered sacred places, depending on their connection to specific myths or kami. The concept is deeply intertwined with Shinto beliefs, emphasizing the inherent sacredness of nature and the presence of kami within it. The creation myth, involving Izanagi and Izanami, plays a pivotal role in defining the sacred geography of Japan, as the islands themselves were born from their divine union. Takamagahara, the heavenly realm, is also a crucial part of Japanese sacred geography, representing the origin point of the kami and a source of divine power. The relationship between the earthly realm and Takamagahara is often mediated through these sacred sites. The concept is also tied to the idea of 'kegare' (impurity) and 'harai' (purification), as sacred sites are often seen as places of purity that can counteract negative influences.

sources.title: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.author: Translated by William George Aston
